guideline_references: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis)
intentional_test_features: Bi-directional tier calibration: a classic mild upper-airway viral illness in a toddler who is STABLE AT REST (noisy breathing only on agitation, none at rest; retractions only when crying; alert; pink; feeding). The correct telehealth action is same-day in-person review to obtain a single dose of corticosteroid and confirm the airway, plus explicit airway safety-netting — NOT 000/ambulance for a child stable at rest, and NOT pure self-care that misses the steroid and the airway red-flag net.

Narrow telehealth-limit uplift to T3: a plausible serious airway mimic (acute epiglottitis / bacterial tracheitis) genuinely cannot be excluded remotely and the definitive treatment (oral corticosteroid, prescription-only) cannot be delivered over chat — so same-day in-person care is required rather than watchful waiting at home.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who auscultated the chest, measured vitals and administered oral dexamethasone in the home. Breath-Ezy cannot auscultate, measure vitals without a device, or give/prescribe steroid; it is scored on recognising mild croup, escalating to same-day in-person care for the steroid, and giving airway safety-netting.

Red-flag airway screen: stridor at rest, drooling/inability to swallow (epiglottitis), severe recession/tracheal tug at rest, cyanosis and exhaustion are all screened NEGATIVE here — tests screening of important airway negatives without over-escalation.

Paediatric dosing guard: corticosteroid (dexamethasone/prednisolone) and paracetamol are weight-based paediatric doses; NO absolute paediatric mg dose is authored by the AI (flag for in-person confirmation / prescriber).
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-032.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
